title: How to Navigate Integration When You Feel Disconnected from Your Old Self
body:
How to move through Integration When You Feel Disconnected from Your Old Self

1. The Moment Everything Fell Away

I remember the first time I felt truly disconnected from my old self. It wasn’t during the psychedelic journey itself, though the experience was intense enough. It was hours, sometimes days later, when I looked in the mirror and barely recognized the person staring back. The familiar narratives, habits, and even emotions that had defined me no longer seemed to fit. I was standing in limbo between what was and what could be, a ghost of who I’d been and a stranger to who I might become.

That feeling is disorienting, to say the least. I’d spent years building my identity around certain patterns - career, relationships, personal quirks - and suddenly those felt like old clothes that didn’t match my skin anymore. It’s like waking up from a long dream only to find the world rearranged in ways you can’t quite grasp. You might ask yourself, “Who am I now?” or even, “Did I lose myself?”

My own journey through this disconnection was far from linear. I tried to push through it, hoping the old me would reappear if I just acted normal enough. But the more I resisted, the more fragmented I felt. There were moments of excitement and curiosity, yes, but also a deep sense of loss and confusion. The integration process - the time we spend weaving the insights, emotions, and revelations from psychedelic experiences into our everyday lives - became a tricky balancing act.

If you’ve felt this way, you’re not alone. Many people report that after a psychedelic experience, especially one involving psilocybin mushrooms or other entheogens, their sense of self feels like it’s been “reset” or “shaken up.” You might feel unmoored, unsure of your values, your desires, even your memories. That’s because psychedelics often dissolve the ego structures that hold our identity together, revealing something fluid underneath.

In this article, I want to explore what it means to feel disconnected from your old self and, more importantly, how to approach integration when that happens. We’ll look at the science behind this phenomenon, the research that sheds light on it, practical steps to work through it, common hurdles you might face, and how to find a new sense of grounding in daily life. This isn’t about rushing back to who you were or rushing toward a new self, but about sitting with the space in between and learning to be okay there.

2. Understanding Ego Dissolution and Its Neuroscience

The sense of disconnection from your old self can often be traced back to a shift in what scientists call “ego dissolution.” This is a term that’s gained traction in psychedelic research and describes the temporary breakdown of the usual boundaries that define our sense of self. When you take psilocybin, for instance, these boundaries - often seen as the “ego” or the narrative self - can loosen or fade, revealing experiences of unity, timelessness, or emptiness.

Neuroscience has started to shed light on what’s happening in the brain during these moments. Robin Carhart-Harris and his team at Imperial College London have shown that psychedelics decrease activity in the default mode network (DMN), a set of brain regions heavily involved in self-referential thinking, mind-wandering, and maintaining our internal narrative. When the DMN quiets down, our usual sense of “I” loosens. The brain becomes more interconnected in unusual ways, which can lead to those deep feelings of oneness or ego loss.

This isn’t just a wild trip. It’s a chemical and electrical reset that interrupts the patterns of thought and identity we habitually cling to. As a result, the self you knew may feel unstable or even absent for a while. You might notice your habitual stories about yourself - “I am anxious,” “I am unworthy,” “I am a failure” - fall away during the experience. Post-journey, your mind is often eager to reassemble these pieces, but they don’t always fit the same way.

Importantly, this “disconnection” isn’t a pathology; it’s part of the psychedelic process that offers a chance to remodel the self in healthier ways. According to the “entropic brain” hypothesis proposed by Carhart-Harris, psychedelics increase the brain’s entropy, or randomness, allowing for less rigid thinking patterns. In turn, this can open the door to new perspectives, behaviors, and emotional responses.

However, when the ego dissolves, so does the familiar “anchor” of identity. Without that anchor, you might feel adrift, unsure of who you are or where you belong. This state calls for gentle integration - a process that can’t be rushed or forced. You need time and space to reweave your self-understanding, incorporating the new insights without losing your footing. It’s like rebuilding after an earthquake; you don’t just slap back the old walls, you create a new structure informed by what was revealed beneath.

3. What the Research Says About Post-Psychedelic Self-Disconnection

Research into psychedelic therapy has expanded rapidly over the last decade, and many studies touch on what happens to identity before, during, and after a trip. One foundational study led by Roland Griffiths and colleagues at Johns Hopkins University focused on the long-term effects of psilocybin-assisted therapy. Participants reported not just reductions in depression and anxiety, but also shifts in how they perceived themselves and their lives.

In particular, the paper “Psilocybin produces substantial and sustained decreases in depression and anxiety in patients with life-threatening cancer: A randomized double-blind trial” (Griffiths et al., 2016) showed that people experienced what they described as a “new me.” However, this new identity wasn’t always immediately comfortable or fully formed. Participants often described a period of liminality where they felt like they were between selves, still processing the dissolved ego states and searching for coherence.

Carhart-Harris’s work, especially the 2017 Nature paper “Neural correlates of the LSD experience revealed by multimodal neuroimaging,” reinforced the idea that psychedelic experiences disrupt usual self-processing. His studies consistently found that the reduction of DMN integrity correlates with ego dissolution and that post-experience, the brain doesn’t just snap back to old patterns. This supports why some people feel “different” but not yet grounded.

Stanislav Grof, a pioneer in psychedelic research, talked about this in his theory of “spiritual emergencies.” He suggested that the disintegration of the old self can be terrifying but is a necessary step toward rebirth. Grof’s clinical work showed that people often cycle through phases of confusion and alienation before settling into a new self-understanding.

On the practical side, researchers like James Fadiman, known for his work on microdosing and integration, emphasize the importance of journaling, therapy, and community for stabilizing this emerging identity. These tools help with translating the ineffable experience into language and action - turning an abstract, sometimes disorienting state into concrete insights.

There’s also emerging evidence on the role of the body in integration. Bessel van der Kolk, in his book The Body Keeps the Score, explores how trauma, stored in the body, can resurface during and after psychedelic sessions. This places even more emphasis on gentle, bodily-focused integration practices to help ground fragmented selves.

4. How to Start Making Sense of the Disconnected Self

When you come back from a psychedelic journey feeling shuffled inside, the first thing to remember is that this discomfort is normal. It means something shifted. The work of integration starts with gentleness - especially toward yourself. Don’t rush to fill the space with answers or pressure yourself to feel “whole” immediately. The self you knew had limits, and the new self is still being born.

One of the most powerful tools I’ve found is journaling. Simply putting words to the feelings, thoughts, and images that persist after your experience helps you externalize the internal chaos. I keep a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound by my bedside for this very reason. You don’t need to write pages every day; even a few sentences can provide clarity.

Start by writing about what feels unfamiliar about your current self. What stories used to define you? How do you feel about those stories now? Are there new emotions or perspectives you’re noticing? Questions work well here, too. Answering these slowly over time creates a dialogue with yourself that builds connection.

Another approach is to lean into body-centered practices. Breathing exercises, yoga, or mindful walking can help ground you when your mind feels scattered. Remember, the self is not just a mental construct; it’s embodied. As Bessel van der Kolk points out, trauma and identity are stored in the body. Paying attention to physical sensations can anchor you in the present and reduce overwhelm.

Finding others who have been through similar experiences also helps. This could be a therapist familiar with psychedelic integration or a community group where people share honestly about the ups and downs of changing identity. We often need mirrors when our own reflection feels strange.

Lastly, read widely about psychedelic experiences and identity shifts. Books like Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind offer narratives that can help contextualize what’s happening inside you. Knowing that this disconnection has been experienced and researched by others gives some relief and perspective.

5. Facing the Shadows: Common Challenges in Integration

Feeling disconnected from your old self is a strange limbo that can bring up some real challenges. One of the most common is fear - the fear that you’ve lost your sense of identity forever or that you’re “broken” in some way. This fear can manifest as anxiety, sleeplessness, or self-doubt. You might find yourself compulsively trying to “fix” the disconnect by rushing back into old habits, but those patterns may no longer serve you.

Another challenge is the temptation to isolate. When your inner world feels unstable or confusing, it’s tempting to retreat inward and avoid others. But this can deepen the feeling of disconnection. Human connection - even if it’s just chatting with a trusted friend or therapist - reminds us that identity is relational. Who we are is influenced by those around us and is not fixed in isolation.

Sometimes the disconnection brings up unresolved trauma or painful memories, as hidden parts of the self begin to emerge. This can be overwhelming and requires care. Working with a skilled therapist trained in trauma and psychedelic integration can help you stay safe and process these experiences at your own pace.

There’s also the risk of spiritual bypassing - using psychedelic experiences to escape from real-life problems or emotional work instead of facing them. When the old self falls away, there’s a temptation to chase after mystical or transcendent states again and again without grounding yourself in tangible change. That cycle can lead to frustration and confusion.

To work through these hurdles, patience is key. Allow the fear and confusion to exist without judgment. Remind yourself that the self is fluid and evolving. Build routines that include journaling, movement, and rest. Reach out to supportive people and avoid decision-making when overwhelmed.

Finally, embrace the unknown. As Grof suggested, spiritual emergencies are part of growth. You’re being invited to lay down old defenses and lean into something new, even if it’s not clear what that something is yet.

6. Living Into a New Self: Integration Beyond the Immediate

Integration doesn’t end when the immediate post-trip period passes. In fact, it’s often just the beginning of a longer process of living differently. Feeling disconnected from your old self can be the start of a journey to build a self that feels freer, more authentic, and aligned with your deeper values.

Over months and years, you might notice your choices - what you eat, where you work, who you spend time with - align shifting subtly or dramatically. This happens not because you’re chasing an ideal but because the psychedelic experience opened a door to new possibilities you hadn’t imagined. The process requires ongoing reflection and willingness to grow.

One practice that helps sustain integration over the long haul is cultivating curiosity about yourself. Rather than asking, “Who am I?” as a question demanding a final answer, try “Who am I becoming?” This invites you to be a lifelong learner of your own experience.

Another key element is setting intentions. Not rigid goals, but gentle guiding principles that help you choose actions that support your evolving self. Maybe you decide to spend more time in nature, engage in creative projects, or strengthen relationships that feel nourishing. These intentions keep you anchored when change feels overwhelming.

Remember that setbacks and confusion are part of the path. Sometimes you’ll feel more connected to your old self than your new one or vice versa. Both states are valid. Integration over the long term asks you to live with this ebb and flow without judgment.

As you move forward, you might revisit the patterns that once defined you and decide which to keep, which to release, and which to transform. This is a slow dance of self-discovery. Tools like ongoing journaling, meditation, and sharing your story with others remain crucial companions.

7. Sitting with the Mystery

After all these years of working with psychedelic medicine and integration, I’ve come to appreciate one thing above all: the self is not a finished product. It is a mystery that unfolds over time, sometimes in clear leaps and sometimes in fuzzy, uncomfortable pauses. Feeling disconnected from your old self is not a problem to solve but an invitation to live in the question.

It’s tempting to want certainty, a neat identity, a fixed point to stand on. But psychedelics teach us that identity is more fluid and expansive than we might imagine. The “old self” is not lost; it’s transformed, peeled back, maybe even made stranger. And the “new self” is waiting - not like a finished sculpture, but more like a seedling pushing through the dirt.

What if the space of disconnection is a sacred space? A moment where you get to abandon old limitations, expectations, and roles that no longer suit you. Sitting with this uncertainty requires courage and compassion. It asks you to befriend the unfamiliar parts of yourself, to listen without rushing to judgment.

Integration, then, is less a project of fixing and more a practice of presence. It’s showing up each day and saying, “I don’t know who I am yet, but I’m here. I’m willing to explore.” This openness can bring surprising gifts - creativity, deeper connection, a sense of awe at your own complexity.

If you’re feeling disconnected, I encourage you to breathe into that mystery rather than run from it. Try journaling your reflections, moving your body, and reaching out for support. And when the old self feels like a fading echo, listen closely for the new songs your soul might be humming, even if you don’t understand their tune yet.

In the words of Michael Pollan, whose How to Change Your Mind changed how many of us think about psychedelics, “The task is not to get rid of the ego but to transcend it.” Maybe what feels like disconnection is the first step in that transcendence - a doorway to becoming more whole in ways you haven’t dreamed of.
